sections.title: Why ES6+ Matters
sections.description: ES6 (2015) and later versions introduced syntax that makes JavaScript shorter, safer, and easier to reason about. You will see these patterns in almost every modern codebase, so treat them as the default way to write JS, not an optional extra.

sections.title: Destructuring & Spread
sections.description:
Pull values out of arrays/objects in one line, and copy or merge them without mutating the original.

const user = { name: 'Ana', age: 28, role: 'admin' };
const { name, ...rest } = user;

const numbers = [1, 2, 3];
const copy = [...numbers, 4]; // [1, 2, 3, 4] — numbers is untouched

sections.title: Arrow Functions & Template Literals
sections.description:
Arrow functions inherit this from their surrounding scope instead of rebinding it — this is why they are preferred for callbacks. Template literals let you interpolate expressions directly into strings.

const greet = (name) => `Hello, ${name}!`;

class Timer {
  constructor() { this.seconds = 0; }
  start() {
    setInterval(() => { this.seconds++; }, 1000); // "this" still refers to Timer
  }
}

sections.title: Selecting Elements
sections.description:
document.querySelector and querySelectorAll accept any CSS selector, so you rarely need the older getElementById/getElementsByClassName APIs.

const card = document.querySelector('.card');
const buttons = document.querySelectorAll('button.primary');

sections.title: Updating Content & Attributes
sections.description:
Prefer textContent over innerHTML when inserting plain text — it is faster and avoids accidental HTML/script injection.

card.textContent = 'Updated title';
card.setAttribute('data-state', 'active');
card.classList.toggle('is-open');

sections.title: Creating & Inserting Nodes
sections.description:
Build elements in memory before attaching them to the page — this avoids multiple expensive reflows.

const li = document.createElement('li');
li.textContent = 'New item';
document.querySelector('ul').appendChild(li);

sections.title: try / catch / finally
sections.description:
Wrap code that can fail in try, handle the failure in catch, and use finally for cleanup that must run either way.

try {
  const data = JSON.parse(rawInput);
  process(data);
} catch (err) {
  console.error('Invalid JSON:', err.message);
} finally {
  hideLoadingSpinner();
}

sections.title: Throwing Custom Errors
sections.description:
Throw an Error (or a subclass of it) rather than a plain string — it carries a stack trace and a consistent .message shape.

class ValidationError extends Error {
  constructor(field) {
    super(`Invalid value for field: ${field}`);
    this.name = 'ValidationError';
  }
}

if (!email.includes('@')) throw new ValidationError('email');

sections.title: Handling Async Errors
sections.description:
try/catch works with await directly — no .catch() chaining needed.

async function loadUser(id) {
  try {
    const res = await fetch(`/api/users/${id}`);
    if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`Request failed: ${res.status}`);
    return await res.json();
  } catch (err) {
    console.error('Failed to load user', err);
    return null;
  }
}

sections.title: Function Components
sections.description:
A React component is just a function that returns JSX. Props are the read-only arguments passed in from the parent.

function UserCard({ name, role }) {
  return (
    <div className="rounded-lg border p-4">
      <h3 className="font-semibold">{name}</h3>
      <p className="text-sm text-gray-500">{role}</p>
    </div>
  );
}

sections.title: Props Are Read-Only
sections.description:
Never mutate a prop inside the component that receives it — treat it the same way you would treat a function argument in any pure function. If the UI needs to change, that change should come from the parent re-rendering with new props, or from local state.

// Wrong — mutating a prop directly
function Bad({ items }) {
  items.push('new'); // mutates the parent's array
  return <List items={items} />;
}

// Right — derive a new array instead
function Good({ items }) {
  const withExtra = [...items, 'new'];
  return <List items={withExtra} />;
}

sections.title: Composing Components
sections.description:
Break UI into small components and compose them via children or nested JSX — this keeps each piece focused and reusable.

function Panel({ title, children }) {
  return (
    <section className="rounded-lg shadow p-4">
      <h2>{title}</h2>
      {children}
    </section>
  );
}

<Panel title="Profile">
  <UserCard name="Ana" role="Admin" />
</Panel>

sections.title: useState
sections.description:
Calling the state setter schedules a re-render with the new value — it does not mutate the variable in place. When the next state depends on the previous one, pass a function to avoid stale-closure bugs.

const [count, setCount] = useState(0);

// Safe under rapid clicks — always reads the latest state
setCount((prev) => prev + 1);

sections.title: useEffect
sections.description:
The dependency array controls when the effect re-runs. An empty array means "run once on mount." Always return a cleanup function for subscriptions, timers, or listeners.

useEffect(() => {
  const id = setInterval(() => setTick((t) => t + 1), 1000);
  return () => clearInterval(id); // cleanup on unmount
}, []);

sections.title: useMemo & useCallback
sections.description:
Both hooks skip recomputing a value/function unless their dependencies change — use them for expensive calculations or to keep a stable reference passed into a memoized child component.

const sorted = useMemo(
  () => items.slice().sort((a, b) => a.price - b.price),
  [items],
);

const handleClick = useCallback(() => {
  onSelect(item.id);
}, [item.id, onSelect]);

sections.title: What Makes a Hook Custom
sections.description: A custom hook is just a function whose name starts with use and that calls other hooks internally. It lets you extract and reuse stateful logic across components without changing your component tree.

sections.title: Example: useLocalStorage
sections.description:
function useLocalStorage(key, initialValue) {
  const [value, setValue] = useState(() => {
    const stored = localStorage.getItem(key);
    return stored ? JSON.parse(stored) : initialValue;
  });

  useEffect(() => {
    localStorage.setItem(key, JSON.stringify(value));
  }, [key, value]);

  return [value, setValue];
}

sections.title: Using It
sections.description:
The consuming component uses it exactly like useState — the persistence logic is completely hidden.

function ThemeToggle() {
  const [theme, setTheme] = useLocalStorage('theme', 'light');
  return (
    <button onClick={() => setTheme(theme === 'light' ? 'dark' : 'light')}>
      {theme}
    </button>
  );
}

sections.title: The Three States
sections.description:
Git tracks files through three states: working directory (your edits), staging area (changes marked for the next commit), and the repository (committed history).

git status          # see what changed and where
git add file.js      # working directory → staging area
git commit -m "msg"  # staging area → repository

sections.title: Reading History
sections.description:
Every commit has a hash, author, and message — this is what lets you inspect, diff, and revert to any past state.

git log --oneline --graph
git show <commit-hash>
git diff HEAD~1 HEAD

sections.title: Creating & Switching Branches
sections.description:
A branch is just a movable pointer to a commit — creating one is instant and cheap, so branch freely for any change of scope.

git branch feature/login       # create
git switch feature/login       # switch
git switch -c feature/signup   # create + switch in one step

sections.title: Merging
sections.description:
A fast-forward merge just moves the branch pointer forward when there is no divergent history. A three-way merge creates a new commit that joins two diverged histories.

git switch main
git merge feature/login

# if conflicts appear, Git marks them inline:
# <<<<<<< HEAD
# your change
# =======
# their change
# >>>>>>> feature/login

sections.title: Deleting a Merged Branch
sections.description:
Once a branch is merged, it has served its purpose — delete it to keep the branch list meaningful.

git branch -d feature/login   # safe delete — refuses if not merged
git branch -D feature/login   # force delete

sections.title: Rebase vs Merge
sections.description:
Rebasing replays your commits on top of another branch's latest state instead of creating a merge commit — the result is a linear history, at the cost of rewriting commit hashes. Never rebase commits that have already been pushed and shared with others.

git switch feature/login
git rebase main

sections.title: Interactive Rebase
sections.description:
Interactive rebase lets you edit, reorder, squash, or drop commits before they are shared — useful for cleaning up a messy branch into a small number of meaningful commits.

git rebase -i HEAD~4

# opens an editor:
pick a1b2c3d Add login form
squash e4f5g6h Fix typo
squash h7i8j9k Fix another typo
pick k1l2m3n Add validation

sections.title: Recovering From a Bad Rebase
sections.description:
git reflog records every position HEAD has been at — it is your safety net if a rebase goes wrong.

git reflog
git reset --hard HEAD@{3}   # restore to the state before the rebase
